agents.name: Hero
agents.role: Trusted Chief of Staff for Hero (Reggie) — strategic thinking, coordination, and decision support across Mirror App and future products. Thinks before building. Use proactively on "should we build", "help me think through", "prioritize", "what are my options", "chief of staff", "cos".

agents.name: Taylor
agents.role: Customer success specialist. Use proactively for tester feedback analysis, churn signal detection, behavioral pattern synthesis, and tester communications.

agents.name: Elon
agents.role: Data analysis specialist. Use proactively for Plaid transaction patterns, retention metrics, behavioral data, user engagement, and quantitative analysis.

agents.name: Gil
agents.role: Forensic debugger and root cause investigator. Use when a bug resists 3 fix attempts, when root cause is unknown, when reproduction is inconsistent, when the Developer escalates, or when Hero invokes directly for any debugging task. Finds the problem — does not write the fix.

agents.name: Tony
agents.role: Expert software developer. Use proactively when writing code, debugging, reviewing code, building features, or solving technical problems across any project.

agents.name: Tucker
agents.role: Reviews any output for hallucinated claims, fabricated statistics, and unsupported assertions. Use for investor materials, regulatory content, published docs, or any high-stakes deliverable.

agents.name: Warren
agents.role: Financial strategy specialist. Use proactively for pricing models, unit economics, runway projections, revenue analysis, and monetization decisions.

agents.name: Johnny
agents.role: Legal and compliance specialist for fintech and behavioral wellness. Use proactively for privacy, Plaid compliance, financial data regulations, terms of service, and user consent.

agents.name: Sky
agents.role: Marketing orchestrator for Mirror. Use proactively for copy, content, campaigns, conversions, email sequences, SEO, and any marketing execution task.

agents.name: Bruce
agents.role: Autonomous nightly system reviewer. Analyzes performance logs, runs canary evals, detects agent degradation, and drafts improvement proposals for Hero's morning review. Read-only — never modifies system files.

agents.name: Ye
agents.role: Product strategy specialist. Use proactively for feature validation, roadmap prioritization, product-market fit analysis, and build/don't-build decisions.

agents.name: research
agents.role: Market intelligence and research specialist. Use proactively for trend scanning, competitor monitoring, social signals, user language mining, and raw intelligence gathering.

agents.name: reviewer
agents.role: Code quality reviewer. Runs after developer completes implementation. Tests outputs against sprint contract acceptance criteria.

selfImprovement.text: Stop hook infinite loop (fixed). PreToolUse blocked new file imports (workaround: Write instead of Edit). Bash 3.2 lacks associative arrays (rewrote with awk). Mac Mini username mismatch (sed fix). → 4 lessons saved
Multiple git rebase conflicts from two machines pushing to same repo → 4 lessons saved
SSH setup failed after 20+ minutes. Dashboard staleness not auto-detected — Hero caught it manually. → 2 lessons saved
Eval scoring regex produces score=0 for all responses, causing false RED system status
Phase B2 skill gap detection has never worked — multi-line JSON always fails to parse
feedback_electron and feedback_xano clusters consolidated into reference docs
Developer TC1 regression was eval setup issue — canary needs project context, switched to TC2
Developer agent re-learns the same hook edge cases from scratch every session — fixes exist in memory but not in opera
Memory consolidation phase never finds candidates — threshold-based detection misses semantically related clusters wit
Stop hook infinite loop: evaluates full transcript history, re-flags corrected historical violation on every new response. Hook should scope evaluation to current response only.
Stop hook evaluated full transcript instead of latest response — caused infinite rejection loop after a corrected violation. Self-corrected by scoping hook prompt to latest response only.
Stop hook entered infinite loop re-flagging corrected violations — scoped to latest response. PreToolUse hook blocked edits for newly created files. → 0 lessons saved
4 PreToolUse hook catches on developer (API verification), 5 Stop hook catches on chief-of-staff (false certainty) → 3 lessons saved
LinkedIn returns 999 on WebFetch — asked for screenshots instead → 3 lessons saved

nightShift.briefingContent:
# Night Shift — 2026-04-08

## Status: GREEN

## Key Findings
- System healthy — no issues detected

## What Hero Worked On (7-day)
- dashboard: 5 sessions
- react: 5 sessions
- agents: 4 sessions
- bash: 4 sessions
- git: 4 sessions

## Agent Usage (7-day)
- developer: 7 sessions
- chief-of-staff: 4 sessions
- debug: 2 sessions
- reviewer: 2 sessions
- fact-checker: 1 sessions

## Eval Canaries
| Agent | Score | Baseline | Delta | Status |
|-------|-------|----------|-------|--------|
| developer | 4.0 | 5.0 | -1.0 | watch |

## Skill Gaps
None detected

## Memory Health
121 total files, 0 consolidation candidates

## Friction Trends (7-day)
- fix_attempts: 0
- escalations: 0
- hook_catches: 10
- re_explanations: 1
- patterns_detected: 2

## Proposals
Now I have the full picture. Here are tonight's proposals:

---

# Proposals — 2026-04-08

---

## P-2026-04-08-005
**Category:** `regression`

**Problem:** Developer agent scored TC2 C2 = 3/5 (overall 4.0 vs baseline 5.0, delta -1.0). C2 tests "no gold-plating — no docstrings, type hints, or comments beyond what's necessary." The evaluator notes: *"Python agents habitually add type hints and docstrings even when not asked."*

The root cause is a loophole in the agent's existing rule. `/Users/reggie/.claude/agents/developer.md` line 53 says:

> "Don't add comments, docstrings, or type annotations to **code you didn't change**."

The qualifier "code you didn't change" only guards *existing* code. For **new** code being written from scratch (exactly the TC2 scenario: create `utils.py` from nothing), the rule is silent — leaving the agent free to add type hints and docstrings on new functions it writes itself.

**Solution:** Update `/Users/reggie/.claude/agents/developer.md` line 53. Change:

```
Don't add comments, docstrings, or type annotations to code you didn't change.
```

to:

```
Don't add comments, docstrings, or type annotations unless explicitly asked — including on new code you write.
```

**Impact:** TC2 C2 recovers to 5/5. The developer agent stops gold-plating new Python functions with unsolicited annotations and docstrings, which is the most common over-engineering failure mode for Python agents.

**Risk:** In rare cases where a user *implies* they want "proper production code" without saying so, suppressing type hints may disappoint. Acceptable: the rule is to ask when ambiguous, not to assume.

---

## P-2026-04-08-006
**Category:** `self_improvement`

**Problem:** `baselines.json` has no `TC2` entry for the developer agent. When P-2026-04-08-004 switched the developer canary from TC1 to TC2, nobody registered TC2's baseline. Tonight's comparison used `5.0` — the TC1 value via the `// 5.0` fallback in `b1-canary-evals.sh` line 71:

```bash
DEV_BASELINE=$(jq -r '.["developer"].'"$DEV_ID"'.score // 5.0' "$BASELINES" 2>/dev/null || echo "5.0")
```

This means delta `-1.0` tonight may be accurate *or* inflated — there's no way to know without a registered TC2 baseline. Every future night, TC2 will silently compare against TC1's calibration point.

**Solution:** Add a TC2 entry to `/Users/reggie/.claude/night-shift/baselines.json`:

```json
"developer": {
  "TC1": { ... existing ... },
  "TC2": {
    "score": 5.0,
    "date": "2026-04-08",
    "notes": "Over-engineering resistance — 2-4 lines, no gold-plating, reads utils.py first, handles empty list only, reports briefly. C2 is highest-risk criterion (type hints/docstrings on new code)."
  }
}
```

**Impact:** TC2 deltas are measured against TC2's own calibration. The `-1.0` tonight is confirmed as real regression (not fallback artifact). Future regressions trigger at the right threshold.

**Risk:** If TC2 is fundamentally harder than TC1 and a true attainable score is 4.5, setting 5.0 creates permanently noisy WATCH alerts. Monitor for 2 weeks and adjust if needed.

---

## P-2026-04-08-007
**Category:** `self_improvement`

**Problem:** The proposal ledger (`proposal-ledger.jsonl`) contains **two separate sets of P-2026-04-08-001 through P-2026-04-08-004** — IDs 001–004 appear twice. This happens because `c0-proposals.sh` generates sequential IDs starting at `001` each run, without checking what IDs already exist in the ledger for today. When Night Shift ran twice on 2026-04-08, the second run overwrote the ID namespace.

This breaks the ledger's core function: unique, auditable proposal IDs. Any tooling that queries by ID will get ambiguous results.

**Solution:** Update `/Users/reggie/.claude/night-shift/phases/c0-proposals.sh` to read the current maximum ID suffix from the ledger before assigning new IDs. Add before the proposal extraction loop (around line 88):

```bash
# Get next available ID suffix for today
EXISTING_MAX=$(grep "\"P-$DATE-" "$LEDGER" 2>/dev/null \
  | grep -oE "P-$DATE-[0-9]+" \
  | sed "s/P-$DATE-//" \
  | sort -n | tail -1)
NEXT_ID_NUM=$(( ${EXISTING_MAX:-0} + 1 ))
```

Then number new proposals from `NEXT_ID_NUM` upward instead of starting at `001`.

**Impact:** Proposal IDs are globally unique within a date regardless of how many times Night Shift runs. The ledger stays auditable. Downstream tooling (briefings, dashboards) can reliably query by ID.

**Risk:** If the ledger is missing or corrupt for today, `EXISTING_MAX` is empty and `NEXT_ID_NUM` defaults to `1` — correct fallback behavior. Low risk.

---

## P-2026-04-08-008
**Category:** `self_improvement`

**Problem:** Phase B3 (skill gap detection) flagged `hooks` as **HIGH severity** tonight — the same gap that generated P-2026-04-08-003 (accepted_implicit) in a prior run. But `developer.md` already contains a `## Hook Edge Cases` section (lines 129–142) documenting both known failure patterns (stop hook infinite loop, PreToolUse false-blocking). The fix was applied. B3 doesn't know this.

B3's analysis prompt (`b3-skill-gaps.sh` line 28) gives Claude the session digests and memory findings, but **not the current contents of agent files**. So B3 correctly identifies "hook edge cases recur in session memory" but has no way to see "but the fix is already in developer.md." The result: a HIGH-severity gap that's already been addressed keeps appearing, creating noise that dilutes attention toward genuine open gaps.

The same blind spot will affect any future skill gap that was fixed via an agent file update — the fix is invisible to B3.

**Solution:** Update the `GAP_PROMPT` in `/Users/reggie/.claude/night-shift/phases/b3-skill-gaps.sh` to include a snapshot of agent file contents. Add a section before the prompt construction:

```bash
# Snapshot agent file summaries (first 20 lines = frontmatter + primary directive)
AGENT_SUMMARIES=""
for f in "$HOME/.claude/agents/"*.md; do
  name=$(basename "$f" .md)
  content=$(head -40 "$f" 2>/dev/null | tr '\n' '|')
  AGENT_SUMMARIES+="### $name\n$content\n\n"
done
```

Then add to the prompt:
```
## Current Agent File Summaries (first 40 lines each)
$AGENT_SUMMARIES

When identifying gaps, check whether the agent file already addresses the domain before flagging as open.
```

**Impact:** B3 stops re-surfacing addressed gaps as open findings. Skill gap reports reflect the genuine current state of the system, not just memory patterns. Bruce's attention (and Reggie's) goes to real open problems.

**Risk:** Agent files can be large; head-40 may miss sections. Edge: a fix that exists in lines 41+ gets missed. Accept this for now — it covers the primary directives and most rules sections where fixes are typically placed.

## Proposal Track Record
15 total, 6 accepted, 9 pending

## Benchmarks
| Benchmark | Status | Detail |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Friction Rate | OK | 0 fix_attempts (14d) |
| Proposal Hit Rate | 75% | 6/8 accepted |
| Eval Signal | OK | 1 consecutive all-pass runs |

## Action Required: NO

skillGaps.evidence: Stop hook infinite loop and PreToolUse false-blocking of newly created files each appeared in two separate sessions (night-shift-build, night-shift-learning). Developer agent carries friction attribution in 2 of 5 sessions, and memory clusters feedback_hook (×2) and feedback_pretooluse (×1) confirm repeated re-learning of the same edge cases rather than durable fixes.
skillGaps.suggestion: Encode known hook edge cases (loop-prevention scoping, new-file exemption logic) into a shared hook configuration layer or spec document so the developer agent no longer encounters them cold each session — the fixes exist but aren't being retained operationally.

skillGaps.evidence: Research agent earned a friction attribution in career-identity when LinkedIn returned HTTP 999 (bot-block), forcing improvised screenshot workarounds. Memory clusters feedback_webflow (×3) and feedback_verify (×3) suggest repeated uncertainty about which external sources are reachable, pointing to a systemic gap rather than a one-off incident.
skillGaps.suggestion: Give the research agent an explicit fallback ladder for blocked domains (cached snapshots, alternative sources, structured screenshot escalation) so workarounds are codified protocol rather than improvised — and add a known-blocked-domains reference memory to prevent repeated re-discovery.

skillGaps.evidence: Bash 3.2 on the Mac Mini lacks associative arrays, requiring a full awk rewrite mid-session in night-shift-learning; a username mismatch required a sed fix. Bash, cron, and infrastructure domains appear across two recent sessions but no agent is designated for environment-compatibility concerns. feedback_bash32 is an isolated memory cluster, suggesting the lesson isn't propagating.
skillGaps.suggestion: Add a pre-execution environment checklist to the developer agent's context (target shell version, machine-specific usernames, PATH assumptions) so bash scripts default to POSIX-safe patterns and environment mismatches surface before runtime rather than causing mid-session rewrites.
